consent: If you consent to participate, please select the first option to begin the study.
consent_2:
IMPORTANT:



Please complete the survey in one sitting, stay on-task, and avoid distractions. 



We have recently noticed an increase in participants starting the task and then idling while doing other things. To measure off-task behavior, we use a mouse-tracking tool that can detect if you leave the task. This tool logs the time you spend on- and off-task during the survey. If this tool identifies that you spent more than 15 minutes off-task, your submission will be rejected. To protect your privacy, we do not record any other information about your browsing activity.



There will also be screening and attention checks during this survey, and your submission may be rejected if any are failed. If you complete this survey more than once, you will not be eligible for the study, and you will not be paid for your participation.



Finally, we use Prolific and Qualtrics bot-detection tools to identify automated or bot-generated responses as well as use of generative AI tools, such as chatGPT. If your responses are flagged as likely generated by a bot or AI, your submission will be rejected. We are interested in learning about your thoughts, feelings, and experiences.

 
 -----------------------------------------------------------------------------


Please check the box below to acknowledge that your submission will be rejected and you will not receive payment if:  


	you spend more than 15 minutes off-task; 
	you fail screening and attention checks;
	your submission is flagged as likely generated by a bot or AI by Prolific and Qualtrics.

research_3b:
As a 52-year-old woman, academic research has profoundly influenced my life, particularly in the realm of health and longevity. For example, recent longitudinal studies on perimenopause and hormone replacement therapy (HRT) have provided me with evidence-based options that were largely unavailable or misunderstood by my mother’s generation. This research has allowed me to manage my health with agency rather than anxiety.
Additionally, in my professional life, research into organizational psychology and lifelong learning has given me the framework to successfully navigate career pivots in a rapidly changing economy. While the constant shift in nutritional or exercise science can occasionally feel overwhelming, the overall impact is positive; I feel more empowered to make decisions about my future based on rigorous data rather than outdated anecdotes or marketing trends. Academic inquiry has essentially provided the roadmap for my second act.

research_3b: Academic research helped me diagnose myself and seek the proper treatment. In my freshman year of college, I got tonsillitis. It was the worst I had ever had and was extremely painful. However, I went to the doctor and it was quickly resolved by antibiotics. It then recurred three weeks later. Again, I got antibiotics and was sent home. After the third time, aj began doing my own research because clearly the antibiotics were not getting rid of it. I discovered that I had chronic tonsillitis which no doctor I'd seen had mentioned being a possibility. I was then educated enough to advocate for proper treatment.
govtfund_1b: I believe it almost entirely depends on the intentions of the research and the accuracy. If academic research is being done to better the lives of the majority of taxpayers, then I support federal funding of it. research_3b:
Growing up in university town, academic research has shaped my life in ways I usually take for granted. On a tangible, positive level, I've been able to participate in local university studies ranging from sleep health to consumer behavior. Not only did these opportunities provide a little extra pocket money, but they also gave me first hand look at how scientific knowledge is built from the ground up. It's incredibly rewarding to know that a few hours of my time might contribute to a peer-reviewed paper that helps solve real-world problem or improve public health guidelines. 
The main downside I have experienced is the sheer frustration of accessibility. So much of this incredible, publicly or institutionally funded research is locked behind massive paywalls.

research_3b: Academic research has been largely positive to me personally since it has influenced my thinking pattern and decision-making processes. From reading the literature, I have learned not to take information for granted but rather to challenge assumptions and seek proof to validate statements. Furthermore, research has enhanced my problem-solving abilities since I can consider various opinions and perspectives to reach a sound decision. Research can be daunting at some points due to the sheer amount of information available and even conflicting views among experts. Overall, however, academic research has increased my curiosity and openness to information.
govtfund_1b: I support the use of government funds raised through taxation in supporting scientific research at institutions of higher learning since many breakthroughs have been made through extended research programs funded through public funds and could otherwise have not happened if left to private corporations. Innovations in medicine, technology, agriculture, engineering, and environmental science have led to significant changes in the lives of many individuals. In addition, institutions of higher learning play an integral role in educating scientists and other experts in various fields. Therefore, research funding helps educate and develop the economy as well. However, there is need for proper monitoring of how such resources are used.
govtfund_2: Government investment in research is ESSENTIAL for scientific progress
